evidence: Runtime dependency abstraction is split into many callback-bag structs with repeated nil-check/forwarding glue (`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go`, `goseg/importer/importer.go`, `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`).

Tests must construct large synthetic runtime assemblies to exercise basic behavior (`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_seams_test.go`, `goseg/docker/orchestration/wireguard_test.go`), indicating high indirection cost for ordinary call paths.

`NewEventRuntime(_ ...EventBroker)` accepts broker input but always creates a new in-memory runtime, while production paths still bind to `DefaultEventRuntime()` globals (`goseg/docker/events/events.go`, `goseg/rectify/rectify.go`, `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go`).

README.md defines contract-governance expectations and references `goseg/protocol/contracts/contracts_test.go` plus a path-aware CI gate, but the reviewed contract source files (`protocol_contracts.go`, `startram_contracts.go`) only declare catalog data and snapshot helpers with no local invariant hooks (e.g., uniqueness/lifecycle assertions) that would fail fast when entries drift.

goseg/system/metrics/metrics.go reads live host paths (`/proc/mounts`, `/dev/disk/by-label`, `/sys/class/hwmon`) and uses real timing (`cpu.Percent(time.Second, false)`), but README quality gates list generic `go test ./...` plus selected integration packages and do not document a dedicated strategy for deterministic metrics validation across host/runtime variability.

Urbit config mutation is implemented through repeated per-section wrappers in both config and orchestration layers (`UpdateUrbitRuntimeConfig`/`Network`/`Schedule`/`Feature`/`Web`/`Backup` in `goseg/config/urbit.go` and mirrored methods in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go`).

Transition handling relies on many near-identical imperative step builders and reducer assignments (multiple `build*Steps` functions in `goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go` and long explicit reducer maps in `goseg/rectify/rectify.go`).

Runtime seam construction uses large callback bags and reflection/unsafe callback merging (`mergeRuntimeCallbacks`, `settableValue` in `goseg/broadcast/collectors/collectors_runtime.go`; many runtime callback structs in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go`) rather than narrower typed ports.

Session lifecycle logic mixes real connection management with synthetic placeholder bookkeeping (`enqueueFakeConnIfNilOrUnconnected` in `goseg/session/connection_registry.go`) while `ClientManager` performs repeated map scans and lock choreography around that storage.

findings.summary: Callback-bag runtimes create high-cost indirection with low domain leverage.
evidence: `RuntimeUrbitConfigOps` and sibling structs define many per-method function fields plus near-identical guard/forward wrappers.
`importerRuntime` carries a very large function-field surface and duplicates this with both `ensure*` validation and `withDefaultsImporterRuntime` merge logic.
Tests recreate large adapter scaffolding (`runtimeContainerTestOps`, `runtimeUrbitTestOps`, etc.) to run focused scenarios, showing abstraction overhead leaking into consumers.
findings.suggestion: Refactor runtime seams into a few cohesive capability interfaces (for example: config store, container lifecycle, event publishing) with required constructor dependencies, then remove per-method callback bags and duplicated `ensure/withDefaults` glue.

findings.summary: Event broker API advertises injection but implementation ignores it and falls back to globals.
evidence: `NewEventRuntime(_ ...EventBroker)` discards provided broker arguments and always returns `newTransitionRuntime(100)`.
Core flows publish/subscribe via `events.DefaultEventRuntime()` directly, so call sites do not use an injected broker boundary.
Rectify tests rely on shared default runtime channels with once-started goroutines, reflecting hidden global coupling rather than explicit dependency wiring.
findings.suggestion: Make `EventRuntime` construction honest: either remove unused broker-injection parameters, or support them and thread an `EventBroker` dependency through rectify/workflow runtimes instead of direct `DefaultEventRuntime()` access.

findings.summary: Several transition wrapper types add ceremony without meaningful policy or reuse.
evidence: `urbitTransitionCommand` and `newShipTransitionCommand` are thin single-field structs whose `Apply` methods just forward to one reducer/update function.
`startramRunningTransition` only assigns one boolean field, while the same package already supports direct mutation via `ApplyBroadcastMutation`.
The extra wrapper types increase type surface and call depth without adding validation, policy, or cross-cutting behavior.
findings.suggestion: Collapse one-off transition command structs into direct function-based mutations or shared typed reducer helpers, and keep explicit wrapper types only where they enforce extra policy/validation beyond assignment.

findings.summary: Contract compatibility metadata changes rely on process policy instead of local executable invariants, making regression detection fragile.
evidence: README.md requires per-contract lifecycle assertions in `goseg/protocol/contracts/contracts_test.go` and CI gating for contract-surface changes.

protocol_contracts.go and startram_contracts.go define mutable catalog specs/snapshots and compatibility metadata, but include no local self-checking mechanism in the reviewed files to enforce uniqueness/lifecycle consistency at definition time.
Because governance is externalized to workflow, metadata drift (e.g., contract ID/version/compatibility inconsistency) is only caught if external tests remain fully synchronized with these files.
findings.suggestion: Add table-driven contract invariant tests tightly coupled to each catalog file (or shared invariant helpers called from per-file tests) that assert ID uniqueness, namespace/action consistency, non-empty IntroducedIn/Compatibility, and snapshot immutability behavior; keep CI gate as secondary protection rather than primary detection.

findings.summary: Metrics collection is tightly coupled to host runtime state without a documented deterministic test strategy, increasing flake and blind-spot risk.
evidence: metrics.go reads live OS/hardware endpoints (`/proc/mounts`, `/dev/disk/by-label`, `/sys/class/hwmon`) and waits real time for CPU usage (`time.Second` sampling).
README backend quality gate does not specify targeted metrics package strategy or matrix conditions for these host-coupled paths.
Current implementation has no injected seams for filesystem/time dependencies in the reviewed file, so deterministic unit validation of failure/edge paths is difficult.
findings.suggestion: Introduce dependency seams for filesystem reads, statfs, and CPU sampler/time source, then add deterministic unit tests for mount parsing, label decoding, scanner/statfs error paths, and temperature-source absence; document required host/integration coverage for metrics in README quality gate.

findings.summary: Urbit config section updates are duplicated across layers, creating a brittle multi-site mutation API.
evidence: `goseg/config/urbit.go` defines section-specific mutation methods plus `UpdateUrbitSectionConfig` type-switch logic for all sections.

`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` repeats the same section fan-out with `UpdateUrbitRuntimeConfig`/`Network`/`Schedule`/`Feature`/`Web`/`Backup` wrappers and fallback closures into `UpdateUrbitFn`.
`goseg/structs/configs.go` already exposes section-level update methods on `UrbitDocker`, but orchestration/config still duplicate section routing behavior instead of sharing one mutation path.
findings.suggestion: Collapse section updates into one typed mutation gateway (for example `UpdateUrbitSection[T]`) owned by config, and have orchestration delegate to that single API instead of maintaining mirrored per-section wrappers.

findings.summary: Transition orchestration is copy-paste heavy, making behavior consistency depend on manual edits across many handlers.
evidence: `goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go` has many similarly shaped builders (`buildHandleLoomSteps`, `buildHandleSnapTimeSteps`, toggle builders, link/unlink builders) that each hand-roll persist/config/restart/error scaffolding.
`goseg/rectify/rectify.go` uses long explicit reducer maps assigning `event.Event`/`event.Value` into specific fields one-by-one, repeating the same mapping structure.
Both files encode transition semantics as repeated imperative snippets instead of declarative transition descriptors, increasing drift risk when adding or changing transition types.
findings.suggestion: Introduce declarative transition specs (event key, target field, optional pre/post hooks) and generate common step/reducer wiring from those specs, keeping only unique side effects in custom handlers.

findings.summary: Large runtime callback bags plus reflective merge logic create high-indirection plumbing with weak compile-time guarantees.
evidence: `goseg/broadcast/collectors/collectors_runtime.go` merges overrides via reflection and `unsafe` (`mergeRuntimeCallbacks`, `settableValue`) instead of typed composition.
`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` defines many nested callback structs for runtime wiring, producing broad constructor/state surfaces.
`goseg/docker/orchestration/container/netdata.go` further adds local runtime-requirement validation methods, indicating duplicated dependency validation patterns across subsystems.
findings.suggestion: Replace generic callback-bag merging with narrow typed interfaces per use-case and constructor-time dependency injection; keep validation in constructors so invalid runtimes are unrepresentable.

findings.summary: Session registry combines real websocket lifecycle with synthetic placeholder entries, obscuring invariants and complicating manager logic.
evidence: `goseg/session/connection_registry.go` inserts fake connections (`enqueueFakeConnIfNilOrUnconnected`) when `client`/`conn` is nil, so storage contains both real and sentinel entries.
`goseg/session/client_manager.go` must repeatedly guard nils and scan both auth/unauth buckets (`findMuConn`, `HasAnyAuthConnection`, `GetMuConn`) to compensate for mixed storage semantics.
The same registry is also responsible for cleanup, transition, and broadcast snapshot preparation, indicating responsibility bundling around non-uniform state.
findings.suggestion: Separate token/session bookkeeping from live connection tracking: store token presence explicitly (without fake `MuConn` entries) and keep websocket collections strictly non-nil/live, with indexed lookup by connection identity.

findings.summary: Test suites reimplement similar global-seam reset and fixture wiring patterns, increasing maintenance overhead and inconsistency risk.
evidence: `goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint_test.go` uses multiple ad-hoc reset helpers (`resetParameterGlobalsForTest`, `resetRouterGlobalsForTest`) and repeated command-capture scaffolding.
`goseg/importer/importer_test.go` and `goseg/exporter/exporter_test.go` each manually snapshot/restore package globals and event drains with similar patterns.
`goseg/startram/state_sync_test.go` repeats save/restore of package-level defaults (`defaultAPIClient`, `defaultConfigService`, syncers), showing shared fixture concerns spread across files.
findings.suggestion: Create shared test harness utilities for seam snapshot/restore and event-drain setup (per package or internal testutil) to reduce repeated fixture code and enforce consistent test state isolation.
